items.title: Warsh holds rates but dot-plot flips 2026 forecast from cut to hike — October odds 61%
items.idea: Kevin Warsh kept rates unchanged at his first FOMC but shifted the dot-plot median 2026 forecast from a CUT to a HIKE (3.4% to 3.8%). PCE inflation forecast was marked up sharply: 2.7% to 3.6%. Nine of 18 Fed members now dot a hike. Day-1 reaction: 2yr +16bp to 4.22%, 10yr +3.5bp to 4.46% (front end did the work, long end hasn't moved yet). Nasdaq -1.3%, S&P -1.2%. The Fed put is declared dead. Warsh also skipped his own dot and may scrap the dot plot entirely.
items.angle: The front end repriced; the long end ($TLT) is the next leg. October hike at 61% is live. With US markets closed today for Juneteenth, Monday is the first real stress test. Favor cash/$SHY, lean against $TLT, trim expensive growth into any bounce.
items.tweetA: Warsh's first dot-plot: the 2026 median flipped from a CUT to a HIKE (3.4% to 3.8%). PCE revised 2.7% to 3.6%. 9 of 18 members want a hike. October odds: 61%. Day 1 move: 2yr +16bp, 10yr +3.5bp. Front end priced it. Term premium hasn't. $TLT Monday.
items.tweetB: The Fed put is gone. Warsh dropped guidance, skipped his own dot, and may kill the dot plot entirely. The front end moved +16bp in one session. The long end barely flinched. That asymmetry is the trade. If you still own duration, the regime just changed.

items.title: Contrarian take: 10yr yield made a significant turn; consensus higher-vol call will age like milk
items.idea: MacroCharts is fading the post-Warsh consensus that higher rates volatility is inevitable. Their models are flagging that the 10yr yield likely made a significant turn — few investors are positioned for this. The long end is rallying while short-term rates stay anchored. They also think the Fed ends up NOT hiking this year once the full data range is incorporated over 6+ months. Under Powell, rates vol was the highest since the 2008 GFC and inflation hit 9% (40-year high). The bar for Warsh to do worse is high.
items.angle: The 48-hour consensus on Warsh = higher rates vol is exactly the kind of flash-consensus that historically ages badly. The author specifically notes that people making this call also called for S&P puts at June lows and long-dated oil at this year's highs. MacroCharts is watching a dollar vs gold update and KOSPI for a potential setup.
items.tweetA: Under Powell: rates vol was the highest since 2008, inflation hit 9%. The worst-managed Fed in modern history set a brutal benchmark. Now everyone extrapolates 48 hours of Warsh movement into higher vol for years. That is not a thesis. That is recency bias.
items.tweetB: MacroCharts models are flashing a significant turn in the 10yr yield. Long end rallying while front end anchors. Their call: the Fed won't hike this year once the full data range comes in. The army of tourists calling for higher rates volatility may be spectacularly wrong.

charts.desc: Charts of 2yr and 10yr yield levels referenced; FOMC statement word count chart. Worth opening.

items.title: Strategy STRC stalls at $82.50 intraday; bitcoin flywheel halted as ATM program paused
items.idea: Strategy's STRC preferred stock — designed to trade near $100 and fund bitcoin purchases through share issuance — fell to an intraday low of $82.50 before closing at $88.59. Volume was 10.7M shares vs a normal 3.4M, one of the highest on record. Below par, Strategy can only raise money at a loss, so the ATM program is halted. The flywheel (sell STRC, buy BTC) is offline. Saylor disclosed he designed the instrument back and forth with AI for a few hours, which spread widely as critics focused on structural fragility.
items.angle: The Saylor flywheel thesis depends on STRC staying above $100. At $88, it's broken for now. This is the first real stress test of the entire capital structure. Worth watching: does STRC recover above $100, or does Strategy need to rethink its funding mechanism entirely? $MSTR implications downstream.
items.tweetA: Saylor designed STRC back and forth with AI for a few hours. Today it hit $82.50 — designed to trade near $100. Below par, the bitcoin flywheel halts. Strategy just stopped buying BTC. Volume was 3x normal. The first real stress test of the whole structure.
items.tweetB: The mechanism: sell STRC above $100, buy bitcoin. Works great until STRC falls below par. Today it closed at $88.59. ATM program halted. The instrument that funded the BTC accumulation is offline. $MSTR investors are pricing a model that just stalled.

charts.desc: Chart of STRC price action referenced in the article.

items.title: Morgan Stanley files cheapest ETH and SOL ETFs at 0.14% with staking included
items.idea: Morgan Stanley filed amended S-1s for spot Ethereum (MSSE) and Solana (MSOL) ETFs at a 0.14% sponsor fee — the lowest in both markets. This undercuts Grayscale's Mini ETH Trust (0.15%) and Franklin Templeton's SOL ETF (0.19%). Both funds include staking via Figment, Galaxy, and Coinbase Canada, with a 5% fee to providers. Their BTC ETF (MSBT) launched in April at the same 0.14% fee and has already pulled $300.7M in net inflows.
items.angle: The fee war that started in BTC ETFs is now hitting ETH and SOL. Morgan Stanley at 0.14% makes every competitor above that fee the expensive option. With staking included, this is not a passive index product — it's a yield-generating instrument competing on cost. The $300.7M MSBT track record gives them instant credibility with allocators.
items.tweetA: Morgan Stanley just filed spot ETH and SOL ETFs at 0.14% — the lowest fee in both markets. Undercuts Grayscale ETH Mini (0.15%) and Franklin SOL (0.19%). Staking included via Figment, Galaxy, Coinbase Canada. Their BTC ETF at same fee pulled $300M in 2 months. Distribution scale wins again.
items.tweetB: The ETF fee war just landed on ETH and SOL. Morgan Stanley at 0.14% means everyone above that is now the expensive option. MSSE for ETH, MSOL for SOL, both with staking built in. Wall Street playbook: anchor on price, bundle the yield, let distribution do the rest.

items.title: Ethereum Foundation loses both co-directors; 8 senior departures in 5 months
items.idea: Hsiao-Wei Wang resigned as Ethereum Foundation co-executive director and board member, following co-director Tomasz Stanczan's earlier departure. At least 8 senior figures have left the EF in the last 5 months. Board member Bastian Aue is stepping into an interim role. Wang cited a sabbatical; Vitalik Buterin acknowledged her decade of contributions. The EF's governance, priorities, and strategic direction are under community scrutiny while Ethereum faces intensifying competition from rival blockchains.
items.angle: The EF governance crisis is occurring at exactly the moment Ethereum needs strategic clarity: ETH/SOL competition is heating up, the ETF wave is arriving, and L2 economics remain unresolved. Leadership vacuum at the foundation level is a tail risk the price hasn't fully priced.
items.tweetA: Ethereum Foundation just lost its second co-executive director in a few months. 8 senior departures total in 5 months. No co-directors remain. Vitalik is still the anchor but the organization around him is hollowing out. This is not normal attrition.
items.tweetB: The irony: ETH just got spot ETFs with staking, Morgan Stanley filed at 0.14%, institutional demand is real. And the Ethereum Foundation has lost 8 senior leaders in 5 months including both co-directors. Governance risk doesn't show up in the price until it does.

items.title: CME sues CFTC over Kalshi perpetual futures; Kalshi at $2B revenue eyes IPO; swaps definition fight goes to court
items.idea: CME sued the CFTC arguing Chair Michael Selig overrode the swap definition when approving Kalshi's perpetual futures as futures rather than swaps. On the same day, the CFTC and SEC jointly sought public comment on defining swaps — addressing ambiguities in Dodd-Frank Title VII. Meanwhile, Kalshi is in early IPO talks with banks after passing $2B in annualized revenue. Senator Gillibrand's son Theodore is also building a dual CFTC/SEC regulated perps exchange backed by Lux Capital.
items.angle: The jurisdictional war over who regulates perpetual futures and prediction markets is now in court. Kalshi passing $2B annualized revenue while fighting for regulatory status is a significant milestone. The regulatory outcome determines whether CME has a genuine competitor or an illegal product to shut down.
items.tweetA: CME just sued the CFTC for approving Kalshi's perpetual futures as futures instead of swaps. On the same day, CFTC and SEC ask for public comment on defining swaps. Kalshi is at $2B annualized revenue and talking to banks about an IPO. This is the jurisdictional fight of the decade.
items.tweetB: Kalshi at $2B annualized revenue. CME suing the regulator that approved them. Senator Gillibrand's son building a rival perps exchange. The prediction market space has graduated from novelty to regulatory battleground. The swap vs future definition question determines who wins.

items.title: IPO and SPAC craze echoes 1999: only 35% of S&P beating the index, $600B passive inflows, SPAC volumes up 2.5x
items.idea: Only 35% of S&P 500 constituents have beaten the index in 2026 — extreme narrow leadership. Eighteen of the top 20 performers in the past two months are in the Tech sector. Indexed fund flows have exceeded $600 billion YTD as FOMO dominates. SPAC mergers: 44 announced in 2026 worth $37 billion vs 33 deals worth $15 billion in the same period last year — a 147% jump in deal value. Rosenberg is calling this a market-top signal comparable to 1999.
items.angle: The concentration + passive inflows + SPAC revival combination is the classic late-cycle cocktail. The $600B passive inflow figure is the one to track — when this reverses, concentrated names get hit hardest. SPACs revive at peak enthusiasm and vanish when sentiment turns.
items.tweetA: 35% of S&P 500 constituents are beating the index in 2026. 18 of the top 20 performers: tech. $600B into indexed funds YTD. SPAC mergers: $37B vs $15B same period last year. Rosenberg's call: this is 1999. The math is hard to argue with.
items.tweetB: The 1999 tells: extreme narrow leadership, $600B passive inflows, SPAC volumes 2.5x year over year. Every mania looks obvious in hindsight. The SPAC revival is the signal nobody tracks in real time — until they appear on the cover of every magazine.

charts.desc: Charts inside the full article — worth opening.

items.title: Oil reverses 80% of wartime run-up; gold sentiment plunges from 100 in January to 0 today
items.idea: Oil prices have reversed 80% of the run-up that occurred during the US-Iran war — the geopolitical risk premium is almost entirely gone. Gold sentiment indicators have collapsed from a reading of 100 in January (extreme bullishness) to 0 today (extreme bearishness). Two of the most crowded consensus trades from Q1 2026 are systematically unwinding. The Q1 narrative was: buy oil on Hormuz risk, buy gold on inflation and dollar weakness. Both are now in reverse.
items.angle: When consensus trades unwind this fast and completely, it creates opportunity in the other direction. Gold at 0 sentiment with central bank diversification still intact and Goldman maintaining a $4,900 target is a setup. Oil at 80% reversal raises the question: has the market overshot in pricing geopolitical calm?
items.tweetA: Oil has reversed 80% of the wartime run-up. Gold sentiment went from 100 in January to 0 today. The two biggest geopolitical trades of Q1 2026 are almost fully unwound. Either the world got dramatically safer — or these were just crowded and the unwind is done.
items.tweetB: Gold sentiment at 0 today after being at 100 in January. That's not a correction. That's a washout. Goldman still has a $4,900 target. Central banks haven't stopped buying. The structural story hasn't changed. This is what a sentiment reset looks like before the next leg.

charts.desc: Charts inside the full article — worth opening.

items.title: China: 72% embodied workforce means AI threatens a smaller share of jobs — but young white-collar workers are panicking
items.idea: ChinaTalk's estimate: ~72% of China's workforce does work requiring physical presence vs 47% in the US. The current AI wave most directly threatens desk-based work, so the share of Chinese jobs immediately exposed is narrower. But the exposed 28% — young white-collar workers who spent their entire lives studying for credentials now at risk — are under severe stress. People's Daily 2025: 71.59% of young Chinese cite AI employment impact as their second-biggest concern after housing costs. Tencent Research: nearly 80% of interviewees fear their professional skills will be devalued by AI. Chinese households spend 17.1% of annual income on education (highest in the world).
items.angle: The US has a higher share of desk workers who are immediately threatened by AI. China has a narrower but highly concentrated anxiety among educated youth who sacrificed everything for white-collar credentials. Different political pressure points, different timelines, different regulatory responses.
items.tweetA: ChinaTalk estimate: ~72% of China's workforce is embodied (physical presence required) vs 47% in the US. The current AI wave targets desk work. China's near-term AI displacement problem is narrower — but the 28% who ARE exposed sacrificed everything for credentials that are now melting.
items.tweetB: 71.59% of young Chinese list AI employment impact as their second-biggest concern after housing. Tencent: 80% fear their professional skills will be devalued. Chinese households spend 17.1% of income on education — the highest in the world — for a guarantee that AI is threatening to void.

charts.desc: Chart of Chinese internet search data showing rise of AI anxiety searches — included in the email. Worth saving.

items.title: Chinese courts rule: AI doing a job cheaper is NOT a lawful reason to fire someone
items.idea: In two publicized representative cases — a Beijing labor arbitration ruling in December 2025 and a Hangzhou court ruling in April 2026 — Chinese legal authorities rejected the argument that AI's ability to do a job more cheaply is by itself lawful grounds for dismissal. Beijing elevated both as precedent-setting, with state media amplification. The Party's current line: encourage AI adoption but require retraining and retention rather than raw replacement. This is the legal framework taking shape as Chinese workers seek protection.
items.angle: This is a fundamentally different regulatory posture from the US (at-will employment). If Chinese courts consistently hold that AI cost savings don't justify firing, Chinese companies face a higher bar for AI-driven headcount reduction. Potential downstream: slower AI productivity gains in Chinese firms, or more sophisticated workarounds such as not hiring vs firing existing workers.
items.tweetA: Chinese courts ruled in two publicized cases: AI doing your job more cheaply is NOT a lawful reason to fire you. Beijing elevated both as representative cases — the legal system is drawing a line. The US has at-will employment. China is choosing a different path.
items.tweetB: The Chinese approach to AI displacement isn't regulation by legislation — it's regulation by case law. Courts elevate a ruling, state media amplifies it, regulators formalize it. Two rulings so far: you can't be fired because AI is cheaper. Watch this pattern extend.

items.title: China: data center apathy vs US NIMBYism — state land ownership removes the opposition vector
items.idea: Field research in Guizhou Province (China's major data center hub) found locals largely apathetic: pretty much no impact on ordinary people like us. Chinese urban homeowners hold time-limited use rights, not freehold ownership. The state owns the land. Village heads cut deals without community consent. There is no mechanism for a single homeowner to block development via legal challenge. The annual Big Data Expo in Guizhou is the local touchpoint with tech. US data center backlash is much larger because the US is building far more facilities — but also because Americans have actual property rights to defend.
items.angle: US NIMBYism around data centers is becoming a real capex constraint for hyperscalers. China has no equivalent friction. This is a structural advantage for Chinese AI infrastructure buildout not captured in GPU count comparisons. The legal and political barriers to data center construction in the US add time, cost, and uncertainty that China doesn't face.
items.tweetA: Why aren't Chinese locals protesting data centers? The state owns the land. Urban homeowners have time-limited use rights, not freehold. Village heads cut deals. Nobody has legal standing to block. In Guizhou, China's data center hub: pretty much no impact on ordinary people like us.
items.tweetB: US data center NIMBYism is a real capex constraint. China has zero equivalent friction — property rights don't allow it. This structural difference doesn't show up in GPU count comparisons. The AI infrastructure race has a regulatory dimension the benchmarks don't capture.

charts.desc: Chart comparing US vs China data center buildout metrics. Worth opening.

items.title: China robot tax debate, Xi rejects UBI, DeepSeek writing 80% of CYL speeches, and the AI crisis of meaning
items.idea: Unitree founder Wang Xingxing proposed a robot tax on AI outputs; opponents argue it would hurt competitiveness. Xi Jinping has personally rejected welfarism and resisted UBI. ~50 million Chinese are on government-funded payrolls and essentially immune to AI replacement (bianzhi quota system). By end 2025, Communist Youth League organizers were using DeepSeek for over 80% of their speeches. The deeper thesis: when the system stops promising guaranteed returns, there is nothing left except endless nothingness and the choices you make for yourself. A Chinese tech writer calls this a crisis of meaning, not a labor crisis.
items.angle: The crisis of meaning framing is distinct from the Western displacement narrative. China's social contract was growth + meritocracy = upward mobility. AI threatens the meritocracy part. Xi's rejection of UBI means there is no institutional safety valve. This creates a political time bomb the Party has no clean solution for.
items.tweetA: Chinese civil servants are using DeepSeek for 80%+ of their speeches — but their jobs are protected by the bianzhi quota system regardless. Xi has personally rejected welfarism. There's no robot tax, no UBI. Just 300 million union members whose union can't confront management.
items.tweetB: China's AI anxiety isn't about jobs. It's about meaning. When the system stops promising guaranteed returns, there is nothing left except endless nothingness. Thirty years of study-harder-get-a-white-collar-job is breaking down. The CCP built its legitimacy on growth. AI is the stress test.

items.title: Goldman cuts gold target but $4,900 is still structurally bullish — correction may have done the cleansing
items.idea: Goldman trimmed its year-end gold target but still forecasts $4,900 — which Dark Side of the Boom frames as a structurally bullish call wearing a tactical caution label. The Street took one hawkish Fed meeting and priced an entire hiking cycle, which looks like recency bias. Speculative positioning has softened, downside hedges are now expensive, and ETF exposure is under-owned. Central bank diversification remains the structural bedrock beneath gold. The author is asking whether Goldman's trim could paradoxically function as a buy signal.
items.angle: When gold sentiment hits 0 (per Rosenberg) and Goldman cuts target while still forecasting $4,900, the contrarian setup is notable. The cleansing thesis: the correction has already compressed positioning and sentiment enough that the next move may be higher. Central bank buying hasn't stopped; the structural USD diversification narrative is intact.
items.tweetA: Goldman trimmed its gold target but still calls for $4,900. That's not a bear call — it's a bull call in a yellow jacket. The Street is pricing a full hiking cycle off one Warsh press conference. Sentiment just went from 100 to 0. Speculative positioning is softer. ETFs are under-owned. This looks like a reset, not a reversal.
items.tweetB: Goldman's tactical gold target cut may be the buy signal. The cleansing work is nearly done: softened positioning, expensive downside hedges, under-owned ETF exposure, gold sentiment at 0. The central bank diversification bid hasn't changed. $4,900 is still the Goldman year-end call.

charts.desc: Charts inside the full article — worth opening.

items.title: ~24 AI model releases since Feb 28 while everyone watched Hormuz; the capability gap compounds
items.idea: Since February 28 — while macro attention was absorbed by the US-Iran war and Strait of Hormuz — there were roughly 24 notable model releases. Anthropic alone: Opus 4.7, Opus 4.8, Mythos Preview, Fable 5/Mythos 5. OpenAI: GPT-5.4 and GPT-5.5. DeepSeek V4 Preview with a 1-million-token context window. Google, xAI, Mistral, Moonshot, MiniMax, Qwen, and NVIDIA all pushed forward. The cadence has gone from every few months to every few weeks. The divide between people using AI to do work vs asking questions is widening into a structural gap.
items.angle: The AI buildout is not correlated with any single geopolitical outcome — it accelerated through the Iran war, through tariff noise, through every macro distraction. The people treating AI as a chatbot are compounding their disadvantage. The investment implication: the buildout thesis doesn't need geopolitical calm.
items.tweetA: While everyone was watching Hormuz from Feb 28 to today, there were ~24 notable AI model releases. Anthropic alone went Opus 4.7 to 4.8 to Mythos Preview to Fable 5. OpenAI: GPT-5.4 and 5.5. DeepSeek V4 with a 1M token window. AI is not waiting for the world to calm down.
items.tweetB: The AI capability frontier is now moving faster than the geopolitical news cycle. The people treating it as a chatbot are 24 model generations behind people building with it. That gap is compounding. The cadence of releases is the signal, not any individual model.

items.title: 2025 HF scoreboard: Citadel Wellington trails S&P at 10.2%; pod model reaches capacity ceiling
items.idea: Global HF AUM closed 2025 at a record $5.15 trillion (+$642.8B, best since 2009). But the largest platforms underperformed: Citadel Wellington returned 10.2% (worst since 2018, vs S&P 16.4%). Citadel is returning ~$5B to investors and trimming to $67B, having distributed ~$25B since 2017. Millennium: +10.5% on $83.5B. Point72: +18%, best among majors; Cohen cleared $3.4B personally. Allocators are reading this as evidence that the flagship pod model has hit a capacity ceiling where each additional dollar of risk decays the return on existing capital.
items.angle: Citadel returning money to investors is the most bearish statement any large platform has made about its own scalability. The historical pattern: once the largest platform admits diminishing returns, allocators shift to smaller, more nimble vehicles. The $10-30B range captured most of the tariff-driven dispersion last year — that's the sweet spot for the next cycle.
items.tweetA: Citadel Wellington: +10.2% in 2025. S&P: +16.4%. The flagship pod model underperformed the index by 6 points and is now returning $5B to investors ($25B since 2017). The allocator read: the pod model has hit a capacity ceiling. Each extra dollar decays the return on dollars already deployed.
items.tweetB: The largest multi-strategy platforms lagged both the index and their smaller competitors in 2025. Citadel returns $5B. Funds in the $10-30B range captured most of the tariff-driven dispersion. This is the rotation out of scale and into nimbleness — and Citadel just confirmed it.

items.title: Bridgewater Pure Alpha II returns 34% (best ever); D.E. Shaw Oculus at 28%; Dalio exits entirely
items.idea: Bridgewater's Pure Alpha II climbed 34% in 2025 — its best result on record — with the All Weather strategy up 20%. Ray Dalio has sold his remaining stake and stepped off the board entirely. D.E. Shaw's Composite returned 18.5%, with its Oculus vehicle at an estimated 28.2%. ExodusPoint returned 18% (best since its 2017 launch). Balyasny posted 16.7%. The funds in the $10-30B range dominated while the largest platforms lagged.
items.angle: Bridgewater's best year on record happening as Dalio exits is historically significant — either the best succession story in hedge fund history or a coincidence. D.E. Shaw's Oculus at 28% is the most interesting number in the table; almost nothing about how it runs gets discussed publicly.
items.tweetA: Bridgewater Pure Alpha II: +34% in 2025. Best year on record. All Weather: +20%. Ray Dalio sold his remaining stake and left the board. The firm he built is having its best year as he walks out the door. That's either the best succession story in hedge fund history or a very interesting coincidence.
items.tweetB: D.E. Shaw Oculus: estimated 28.2% in 2025. ExodusPoint: 18%, best since 2017 launch. Balyasny: 16.7%. The funds nobody talks about are having the best years. Meanwhile Citadel trails the index and returns $5B. Alpha is migrating to the platforms not on the magazine covers.

items.title: Marginal HF alpha has shifted to engineering and compute; the single largest macro position is a bet on data center construction
items.idea: Young & Calculated reports that marginal alpha in quantitative finance has shifted away from pure research toward engineering capacity and compute procurement. The single largest macro position across the hedge fund book right now is a financed bet on data center construction. Roughly 47% of mid-to-large hedge funds had at least one generative AI system running in production by Q1 2026, concentrated in systematic and multi-strategy firms above $5B AUM. Machine learning is now described as the cost of entry, not an edge.
items.angle: The largest hedge funds are collectively long data centers — that is the macro position. This creates a self-reinforcing dynamic: HFs need compute, HFs bet on compute buildout, compute buildout attracts more HF investment. The 47% adoption number at the top of the industry signals near-saturation; the edge is now in who runs the infrastructure, not who has the model.
items.tweetA: The single largest macro position across the hedge fund book right now is a financed bet on data center construction. 47% of major HFs have AI in production already. ML is now the cost of entry. The edge has shifted from research to engineering capacity and compute procurement.
items.tweetB: The biggest HF macro trade isn't a rate bet or a currency call. It's a bet on data center construction. The smartest risk capital in the world is most concentrated in AI infrastructure. They are not just users of AI — they are its largest financial backers.

items.title: AI agents gain Visa network access via Alchemy; HIVE bitcoin miner inks $220M AI GPU deal with Bell for Cohere
items.idea: Alchemy's AI-driven identity and payment service gained access to the Visa network, enabling AI agents to transact on traditional payment rails through Visa-powered virtual cards. Separately, bitcoin miner HIVE signed a $220M sovereign AI GPU contract with Bell for Cohere, expecting ~$70M in annual revenue. This continues the trend of bitcoin miners pivoting spare capacity toward AI infrastructure. HIVE's Buzz HPC subsidiary is the contracting entity.
items.angle: Two different but related infrastructure plays: (1) AI agents getting payment rails means autonomous AI commerce is no longer just a concept — it's on Visa. (2) The BTC miner to AI GPU pivot narrative is maturing from press releases to $200M+ multi-year contracts. HIVE's deal is among the largest executed so far.
items.tweetA: Alchemy's AI payment service just gained Visa network access. AI agents can now transact on traditional payment rails through a Visa-powered virtual card. Autonomous AI commerce just went from white paper to infrastructure. The payment layer for AI agents is being built quietly.
items.tweetB: Bitcoin miner HIVE just signed a $220M AI GPU contract with Bell to power Cohere — $70M expected annual revenue. The BTC miners pivot to AI playbook is no longer theoretical. These are nine-figure multi-year infrastructure deals. Cheap power plus idle racks plus AI demand equals the new mining thesis.

items.title: GENIUS Act stablecoin KYC rules proposed; Algorand targets quantum resistance by 2028
items.idea: Federal regulators (FinCEN and agencies) proposed a rule requiring stablecoin issuers to maintain bank-style customer identification programs under the GENIUS Act. This is among the first concrete implementing rules for the stablecoin law. The compliance burden is structured like banking KYC requirements — which smaller issuers may not be able to absorb at cost. Separately, Algorand published a phased roadmap to reach quantum resistance by 2028, joining a small group of networks treating post-quantum security as a near-term engineering priority rather than a distant concern.
items.angle: The GENIUS Act KYC rule is textbook regulatory consolidation: write rules that incumbents can absorb (USDC, USDT) but that compress smaller competitors. Circle and Tether just got a structural moat handed to them by compliance cost. The Algorand quantum roadmap is a longer-term watch — 2028 is close enough to matter if quantum computing timelines accelerate.
items.tweetA: The GENIUS Act's first real implementing rule: bank-style KYC for stablecoin issuers. The burden lands on compliance cost — which small issuers can't absorb. Regulatory compliance is always a moat for incumbents. Circle and Tether just got a structural advantage handed to them by rulemaking.
items.tweetB: Algorand just published a phased roadmap to quantum resistance by 2028. Small number of blockchains treating this as a near-term engineering problem rather than a distant concern. If quantum timelines compress, being the first quantum-resistant L1 is a genuine differentiator.

items.title: Post-Warsh regime: strengthening USD, constructive equities, explicit caution on Bitcoin, crypto, and gold
items.idea: Cubic Analytics' post-Warsh thesis (from a 62-minute premium livestream): the main theme is regime change at the Fed, a strengthening US dollar, still-constructive equity trends, and explicit caution toward Bitcoin, crypto, gold, and other non-cash-flow assets. The hawkish, independent Warsh Fed supports the USD. If the dollar strengthens from here, assets that historically benefit from dollar weakness face structural headwinds. This is a coherent macro framework that links the Fed regime change to specific asset class implications.
items.angle: The non-cash-flow asset caution is the key call. Bitcoin, crypto, and gold are all unified by their sensitivity to dollar direction and real rates. A Warsh Fed that's hawkish and independent of political pressure is a headwind for all three simultaneously. Constructive on equities overall — but only the cash-flow-generating kind.
items.tweetA: Cubic Analytics post-Warsh framework: strengthening USD, constructive equities, explicit caution on Bitcoin, crypto, and gold. The logic: hawkish independent Fed equals dollar bid. Dollar bid equals headwind for non-cash-flow assets. Three seemingly different asset classes, same underlying exposure.
items.tweetB: The Warsh regime change creates a unified headwind: if the Fed is genuinely hawkish and independent, the dollar strengthens. If the dollar strengthens, Bitcoin, gold, and crypto face the same pressure simultaneously. That's not three separate calls — it's one macro view with three expression points.
